question: A school bus ahead stops with its red lights flashing. You must:
choices: Slow to about 10 mph and ease past the bus carefully as long as you watch closely for any children stepping into the roadway
Stop only if you are right behind the bus, since drivers approaching from other lanes stay free to keep going
Make a complete stop no matter where your vehicle is positioned — same lane, opposing lane, or at an intersection — until the red lights are turned off
Move into the left lane and pass the bus at a steady speed whenever no children happen to be visible getting on or off
explanation: When a school bus's red lights flash, ALL traffic must make a complete stop and never pass in any direction until the bus finishes loading/unloading and moves on with its lights off.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 12 — School Bus Law and Rules

question: You are NOT required to stop for a stopped school bus only when:
choices: You are running late and the road ahead is clear enough to pass
No children are visible outside the bus and its door looks closed
You are stopped at an intersection on a cross street near the bus
The bus is in the opposite lanes of a road separated by a median 20 feet or more wide
explanation: The only exception: the bus is traveling the opposite direction on a roadway separated by a median at least 20 feet wide. Everyone else — including traffic at intersections — must stop completely.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 12 — School Bus Law and Rules

question: Why should you avoid lingering beside a large truck on the highway?
choices: Trucks have large blind-spot 'No-Zones' — up to 20 feet in front, along both sides, and up to 200 feet behind
Driving beside a commercial truck is illegal in Arkansas and earns a citation
Large trucks tend to drift unpredictably between lanes, so it is risky
Truck drivers cannot use their side mirrors, leaving them fully blind to cars
explanation: If you can't see the truck driver in the truck's side mirror, the driver can't see you. Pass completely and on the left, and never cut in close — a loaded truck at 55 mph needs over 335 feet to stop.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 64-66 — Tractor-Trailer No-Zone

question: You are overtaking a bicyclist on a two-lane road and an oncoming car is approaching. You should:
choices: Squeeze through the gap between the bicycle and the oncoming car at a steady speed
Slow down, let the oncoming vehicle pass, THEN pass the bicycle with plenty of room
Sound your horn firmly so the cyclist understands to move over onto the shoulder and let you continue past without slowing
Follow close behind the cyclist at their pace until they leave the road
explanation: Never pass between a bicyclist and oncoming traffic. Take hazards one at a time: let the oncoming car pass first, then give the cyclist plenty of room. Never pass where the street is too narrow.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 67 — Sharing the Road with Bicycles

question: A police car is stopped on the shoulder ahead with its lights flashing. Arkansas's Move Over law requires you to:
choices: Come to a complete stop behind the police car and wait there until the officer waves you forward past the scene
Keep driving at the posted speed in your lane, since it is off the road
Move to the farthest lane away from it whenever possible, or slow down if you can't change lanes
Switch on your hazard lights and then pass the stopped police car quickly
explanation: On a multilane highway you must move over to the farthest lane away from a stopped law-enforcement or emergency vehicle whenever possible. Violations carry fines up to $1,000, possible jail, and up to a 6-month license suspension.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 11 — Move Over Law

question: Motorcycles are entitled to:
choices: Half a traffic lane width
Use of the paved road shoulder only
Just the right third of a travel lane
A full lane width, just like a car
explanation: Never share a lane with a motorcycle — it needs the full lane width to maneuver. Allow 4 to 6 seconds of following distance behind a motorcycle, and look twice for them before turning, especially left turns.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 70 — Respect a Motorcycle

question: At a railroad crossing, the gates begin to come down just as you start to cross. You should:
choices: Keep going — even if it means you will break the gate
Stop and wait on the tracks until the gate lifts
Shift into reverse and back off the crossing
Sound your horn and wait on the tracks for it
explanation: Never stop on the tracks. If gates start lowering while you're crossing, keep going even if you break the gate. Never drive around lowered gates, and remember a second train may come on another track.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 29 — Gates and Lights

question: The most common car-vs-motorcycle crash happens when:
choices: A motorcycle following too closely rear-ends a car ahead
A car driver makes a LEFT TURN in front of an oncoming motorcycle
A motorcycle illegally passes slower cars by riding up the shoulder
A car backs out of a space into a parked, empty motorcycle
explanation: Nearly 60% of motorcycle intersection crashes are caused by another vehicle turning left in front of the rider. Motorcycles are small and their speed is hard to judge — always take a second look before turning left.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 69 — Situations When Crashes Are Most Likely

question: School buses are required to stop:
choices: Only at gated crossings
Only with kids aboard
At all railroad crossings
Never, having priority on tracks
explanation: School buses (like transit buses and hazmat trucks) must stop at ALL railroad crossings — leave extra following distance so you aren't surprised.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 12, 28 — School Buses / Railroad Crossings

question: A tractor-trailer ahead of you signals a RIGHT turn but swings out to the LEFT first. You should:
choices: Stay back — trucks must swing left to make right turns, and squeezing in beside them is the 'squeeze play' that causes crashes
Speed up and pass it on the right side before it begins turning, since the gap on its right is the safest place to be
Sound your horn over and over so the truck driver keeps the rig inside its own lane instead of swinging it wide to the left and turning
Pass the truck on the left shoulder while it is making the turn, because that shoulder gives you plenty of clear, open room to get by
explanation: A long vehicle's rear wheels track inside its front wheels, so truck drivers swing left before turning right. Never slip into the gap on the truck's right.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 64-65 — Tractor-Trailer Turning

question: After passing a large truck, you should return to the lane in front of it only when:
choices: Your rear bumper has just cleared the truck's front bumper
The truck driver flashes the headlights at you, which is the accepted signal that it is now safe to merge back over
You have a lead of about two seconds, the normal gap behind a car
You can see the truck's whole front in your rear-view mirror — then don't slow down
explanation: Trucks need nearly twice the time and room to stop. Don't cut in until you can see the entire front of the truck in your mirror — and keep your speed up once you're there.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 65 — Front No-Zones

question: A bicyclist may legally take the FULL lane when:
choices: Never, since Arkansas law requires every bicyclist to ride on the shoulder
Only while riding on residential streets, since cyclists must otherwise stay tight to the right edge on every single other type of road
The lane is too narrow to share safely, when passing, preparing a left turn, or avoiding hazards like parked cars and debris
Only inside lanes that are specially striped and marked as bicycle lanes, and never within any ordinary, unmarked travel lane
explanation: Bikes have the same road rights as cars. A cyclist away from the right edge isn't being careless — they're avoiding car doors, debris, or setting up a left turn. If the lane is too narrow to share, it's safest to let them have it.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 68 — Lane Positions for Bicycles

question: A bicycle has a red or orange pennant flag attached. This indicates:
choices: A competitive racing cyclist
The rider has impaired hearing — slow down
An oversized or extra-wide bicycle load
A bicycle that is being offered for sale
explanation: A red or orange pennant flag on a bike signals a hearing-impaired rider. They can't hear you coming — slow down and give extra room.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 68 — Residential Areas Are Danger Zones

question: When following a motorcycle, allow a following distance of at least:
choices: 4 to 6 seconds
1 second
2 seconds
About 10 to 12 seconds
explanation: Allow 4-6 seconds behind a motorcycle. In dry conditions motorcycles can stop faster than cars, and the slightest contact can mean a spill for the rider.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 70 — Allow Plenty of Space

question: When should you NOT use your horn?
choices: When a child is about to dart from the curb into the street ahead
When another vehicle is drifting toward you and is in real danger of striking the side of your car at any moment
While approaching a blind hill or a sharp curve where an oncoming driver would have no way of seeing your vehicle
At blind pedestrians, to greet a friend, or to scold another driver's mistake
explanation: Use the horn to warn of danger. Never honk at blind pedestrians (it confuses them), to rush or greet people, or to criticize other drivers.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 52 — Warning Horn

question: You are following a truck and cannot see its rear-view (side) mirrors. What does this mean?
choices: Nothing important at all, because modern trucks rely on rear-facing cameras that let the driver watch the traffic behind
You are positioned at a perfectly safe and legal following distance, so the truck's mirrors simply do not need to include you at all
The truck driver has no way of seeing you — you're in the rear No-Zone, which extends up to 200 feet behind the truck
The truck is now required by law to yield the right-of-way to you because you are following directly within its rear blind area
explanation: If you can't see the truck's mirrors, the driver can't see you. The rear No-Zone reaches up to 200 feet — and tailgating a truck also blinds YOU to what's ahead of it.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 65-66 — Tractor-Trailer No-Zone

question: Many car-truck collisions happen at intersections because car drivers:
choices: Honk impatiently at trucks waiting in the intersection, which startles the driver into pulling out far too soon, before it is actually safe to go
Don't realize how close the truck is or how quickly it is approaching — a tractor-trailer's size makes it appear slower than it really is
Follow large trucks far too closely through the intersection and then rear-end them whenever the truck driver happens to brake suddenly or unexpectedly
Cannot see the road over the high hood of their own vehicle and therefore completely fail to notice a large truck as it enters the intersection
explanation: Never underestimate an approaching tractor-trailer: its size makes its speed look slower than reality. When unsure, wait — don't pull out in front of a truck.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 66 — Maneuverability

question: Crossing behind a truck that is backing up is:
choices: Extremely dangerous — truck drivers have no rear-view mirror and may not see you cut in behind them
Perfectly fine as long as you move briskly across, whether you are on foot or driving, before the truck rolls back
Allowed the moment the truck's backup alarm goes silent, since that beeping is the only warning the driver provides
Allowed only within parking lots, where the lower speeds make it safe to slip behind a truck that is backing up
explanation: Never cross behind a backing truck. Hundreds of motorists and pedestrians are killed or injured every year ignoring trucks backing up — the driver cannot see directly behind.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 65 — Backing Up No-Zone

question: What is the most common cause of a collision between a car and a bicycle?
choices: A bicyclist ignoring a red light and rolling straight through a busy intersection into the path of cross traffic
Bicyclists riding after dark without proper reflectors or lights, leaving drivers unable to see them until too late
Two bicyclists riding side by side, which forces drivers to swerve wide around them and into the next lane over
A driver turning left in front of an oncoming bicycle, or turning right across the bicycle's path
explanation: The most common car-bike collision is a driver turning across the cyclist's path — left in front of an oncoming bike, or right after overtaking one. Look for bikes and judge their speed before every turn.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 67 — Safety Tips for Motor Vehicle Drivers

question: Why do bicyclists ride away from the right edge when passing parked cars?
choices: To annoy and slow the drivers stuck behind by hogging the lane
Because Arkansas law requires cyclists to ride dead center there
To draft in the slipstream behind moving cars and save effort
To stay clear of opening car doors and people stepping out — it's proper riding, so give them the room
explanation: Cyclists move toward the lane's center near parked cars to avoid the 'door zone.' A cyclist away from the edge isn't careless — give them the entire lane if possible.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 67 — Sharing the Road with Bicycles

question: A child is riding a bicycle near the road ahead. You should expect that the child:
choices: May make sudden moves or direction changes — children can't properly judge traffic conditions
Will carefully obey all of the traffic rules and signals exactly the way an experienced adult driver would expect
Will reliably stop and look both ways before crossing every driveway
Can hear your car coming from behind and will move safely out of your way
explanation: Children on bikes create special problems: they may dart from driveways, ride mid-street, or disregard signs. Slow down in neighborhoods and expect the unexpected.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 68 — Residential Areas Are Danger Zones

question: A strong gust of wind can move a motorcycle:
choices: Only a couple of inches, since its weight holds it firm against wind
Across an entire lane if the rider isn't prepared — including gusts from large trucks passing in the other lane
Sideways only above 70 mph, since slower riders feel no wind at all
Sideways only when the rider is traveling above 70 mph, since riders going any slower than that are unaffected by the wind
explanation: Wind — including blasts from passing trucks — can push a motorcycle a full lane's width. Give riders extra space in gusty conditions.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 69 — Strong Winds

question: A bicyclist extends their LEFT arm out with the forearm bent UP at the elbow. This signals:
choices: A left-hand turn
An upcoming stop
A right turn
Debris on the road
explanation: Bicycle hand signals: left arm straight out = left turn; left arm bent up = right turn (riders may also point straight out with the right arm); left arm bent down = stop.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 68 — Bicycle Hand Signals

question: Roughly what share of car/motorcycle crashes are caused by the CAR driver turning in front of the motorcycle?
choices: Only about 5 to 10%
Roughly one-quarter
Almost none of them
Over two-thirds
explanation: Research shows over two-thirds of car/motorcycle crashes result from a car driver turning in front of the motorcyclist. Look twice — especially before left turns at intersections.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 69 — Sharing the Road with Motorcyclists

question: A driver convicted of illegally passing a stopped school bus with its red lights flashing faces:
choices: A misdemeanor — a fine of at least $250 (up to $1,000), up to 90 days in jail, or both; and a felony if it causes a death (Isaac's Law)
Only a verbal or written warning for a first offense, with no fine and no jail time at all unless the driver happens to do it again later
Nothing more than a set of points added to the driver's record, which fall off automatically after a year or two
A simple $50 traffic ticket that can be paid by mail, the same as an ordinary minor moving violation in the state
explanation: Passing a stopped school bus with red lights flashing is a misdemeanor: a minimum $250 fine (up to $1,000) and up to 90 days in jail. Causing a death by violating this law is a felony under Isaac's Law.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 13 — School Bus Law and Rules

question: A school bus stops for passengers on a two-lane road, or on a four-lane road with NO median dividing it. Traffic must stop:
choices: Only behind the bus
From BOTH directions
In the bus's lane
Only with kids out
explanation: On a two-lane road (with or without a center turn lane) and on a four-lane road without a median, all traffic in BOTH directions must stop. Only on a divided highway with a median do oncoming drivers not have to stop.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 13 — School Bus Law and Rules

question: You are following a motorcycle on a road with wet pavement, gravel, or a metal surface like a bridge grating or railroad tracks. You should:
choices: Follow closer than usual, since a motorcycle takes longer to stop
Pass the motorcycle right away on these surfaces, before the rider has a chance to slow down and block your path
Allow an extra cushion of space — these surfaces increase the rider's chance of falling
Expect the rider to pull off onto the shoulder and let you by whenever the pavement turns wet, gravelly, or metal
explanation: Allow an extra cushion behind a motorcycle. Wet, icy, gravel, or metal surfaces — bridges, gratings, streetcar and railroad tracks — increase the chance a rider could fall, so give even more room.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 59 — Following Motorcycles

question: On the road, a bicyclist:
choices: Must always ride up on the sidewalk rather than in the street, keeping completely clear of every motor vehicle lane
Has no legal right to use a regular travel lane and must give way to cars by moving onto the shoulder right away
Must yield to every car at all times and on every road, even when the cyclist plainly reached the intersection first
Has the same rights and responsibilities as a driver and must obey the same traffic laws
explanation: Bicycles have the same road rights as cars and must follow the same traffic laws. Treat a cyclist as you would another vehicle, and give them room.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 67 — Sharing the Road with Bicycles

question: About how far does a loaded tractor-trailer traveling 55 mph need to come to a complete stop?
choices: More than 335 feet — about the length of a football field
About the same distance as a passenger car, roughly 225 feet
Only about 100 feet, shorter than a normal car needs
Much less distance than a car needs, since trucks can brake fast
explanation: A loaded tractor-trailer at 55 mph needs more than 335 feet to stop — far more than a car's ~225 feet. Never cut in close in front of a truck and then slow down.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 64 — Tractor Trailer Rigs (Braking)

question: Why is it especially important to look carefully for motorcycles before changing lanes or turning?
choices: Motorcycles must by law yield to every car, so no need to check
Motorcycles are capped by law well below the speed limit and predictable
Their small size makes them hard to see, and it is hard to judge how fast they are approaching
Motorcycles may not use a full lane and must hug one narrow edge
explanation: A motorcycle's small size makes it hard to see and its speed hard to judge. Look twice — especially before left turns and lane changes, where most car/motorcycle crashes happen.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 69 — Sharing the Road with Motorcyclists
